messages.content:
Paul has decided what he's going to do in his life. Complete the sentences, using
short forms of be going to and the verbs in brackets ( ).
I'm
going to study
(study) music at university.
1
(travel) all over the world.
2
(not/work) in an office.
3
… (marry) a very rich woman.
4 We
(have) eleven boys.
5 They
… (become) a football team.
6 They
(win) the World Cup.
7
(play) the piano every night in a cafe.
8 My wife
(not/cook) or clean.
9
We
… (eat) in restaurants every day.

1. I'm (travel) all over the world.
   I'm going to travel all over the world.

2. I'm (not/work) in an office.
   I'm not going to work in an office.

3. I'm (marry) a very rich woman.
   I'm going to marry a very rich woman.

4. We're (have) eleven boys.
   We're going to have eleven boys.

5. They're (become) a football team.
   They're going to become a football team.

6. They're (win) the World Cup.
   They're going to win the World Cup.

7. I'm (play) the piano every night in a cafe.
   I'm going to play the piano every night in a cafe.

8. My wife's (not/cook) or clean.
   My wife's not going to cook or clean.

9. We're (eat) in restaurants every day.
   We're going to eat in restaurants every day.

Positive sentences with short forms of "be going to":
1. She's going to buy a new car tomorrow.
2. They're going to work hard this year.
3. It's going to rain this afternoon.

Negative sentences with short forms of "be going to":
4. Paul's not going to drive to Scotland.
5. We're not going to finish it today.
6. She's not going to buy a new house.

Questions with "be going to":
7. Are they going to win the match?
8. Is Mary going to leave her job?
9. Are you going to take the exam in June?

Keiko is Japanese. She's going to spend a week by the sea in England. Ask her
some questions. Use be going to, the verbs in brackets ( ), and the words in the box.
an umbrella
in the sea
in a luxury hotel
a lot of English
to a disco
golf every day
fish and chips
((speak) Are you.going.to.prick.a lot.of.English?…
1 (play)
2 (take)
3 (swim)
4 (eat)
5 (stay)
6 (go)

1. (play) Are you going to play golf every day?
2. (take) Are you going to take an umbrella?
3. (swim) Are you going to swim in the sea?
4. (eat) Are you going to eat fish and chips?
5. (stay) Are you going to stay in a luxury hotel?
6. (go) Are you going to go to a disco?

Match the words in the box with the pictures, and write a sentence using short forms
of be going to.
It/rain
They/eat/a pizza
They/not/play/tennis
He/not/win/the race
She/have/a swim
They/watch/a film
He/make/a phone call
He/play/the piano
CINEMA

As I don't have access to the pictures you're referencing, I'm unable to match words directly to them or determine which word relates to which picture. However, I can still show you how to write sentences using the phrases you've provided combined with short forms of "be going to". Here are the sentences you might write, based on each phrase:

1. It's going to rain.
2. They're going to eat a pizza.
3. They're not going to play tennis.
4. He's not going to win the race.
5. She's going to have a swim.
6. They're going to watch a film.
7. He's going to make a phone call.
8. He's going to play the piano.

For "CINEMA", you might say something like "We're going to the cinema." If it was supposed to be matched with one of the phrases, such as watching a film, then: "They're going to watch a film at the cinema." If you can describe the pictures or provide more detail, I could tailor the sentences more specifically to each.

4.

A: Are you going to watch TV tonight?
B: Yes, I'm going to watch my favourite programme at nine o'clock.

1.
A: What are you going to eat tonight? What food have you bought?
B: I haven't bought any food.
A: Well, why don't you come to my house? I'll cook us something nice to eat.

2.
A: I'm going into the centre of town tomorrow. I'm going to buy some new clothes.
B: Oh, what are you going to get?
A: I'll look for a T-shirt and some jeans. B: I'd like to go into the centre too. I'll come with you.

3.
A: I'll leave work late tomorrow. There is a meeting at six p.m.
B: Oh, I didn't know that. Well, I'll see you after the meeting.

4. A: I'll phone Tom at six o'clock. I promised to phone him this evening.
B: (This prompt is incomplete, but based on the given sentence, the reply could be:) That's good. He'll be expecting your call.

5. A: Are you going to have a holiday in the summer?
B: Yes, I'll travel around Europe with a friend.

A: Hi, nice to see you. Would you like a drink?
B: I'll have a coke, please.

1.
A: What are you doing these days?
B: Nothing much, but I'm going to start a new job soon.

2.
A: Would you like something to eat?
B: Thanks, I'll have a sandwich.

3.
A: What are your plans for the weekend?
B: I'm going to do some shopping tomorrow and go for a swim on Sunday.

4.
A: Why is Maria standing by the piano?
B: She's going to sing, I'm afraid.

5.
A: This cake looks delicious, Are you going to have some?
B: No, but I'm sure you'll enjoy it.

6.
A: How are you getting home?
B: David's going to give me a lift.

the box. Use short forms of the Present Continuous for the future.
l/eat/in a new restaurant tonight
I/fly/to Florida in August

I/go/to the doctor tomorrow
I/study/English in London in May
I/see/Mary this weekend

1. (You booked a table at a new restaurant last week.)
   I'm eating in a new restaurant tonight.

2. (You bought a ticket for a concert last month.)
   I'm going to a concert next Tuesday.

3. (You telephoned your doctor this morning.)
   I'm going to the doctor tomorrow. 

For the remaining statements, here are some sentences as if the brackets indicated past events:

- (You arranged your flight to Florida recently.)
   I'm flying to Florida in August.

- (You made arrangements to see Mary.)
   I'm seeing Mary this weekend. 

Please note the original prompt refers to the Present Continuous for future plans, which often corresponds to arrangements made for future events, indicated by past actions.

Without access to the pictures you're referring to, I can create sentences based on the information you've provided. To match them with specific pictures, you would need to describe those pictures or tell me which activity goes with which month.

Here are general sentences using the Present Continuous to describe Mark's activities next year, with hypothetical months assigned to each activity:

In January, he's driving across the Sahara.
In March, he's walking across the Antarctic.
In May, he's running across Africa.
In July, he's flying over the Amazon.
In September, he's climbing Mount Everest.
In November, he's sailing across the Pacific.

Please adjust the sentences to match the correct months with the activities according to the information or pictures you have.

Steve: Are you doing anything this weekend?

1. Pete: Jane, Joe, and Sally are coming to my house on Friday night.
   Mark: Are you having a party?
   Pete: No, we're not.

2. David: We're playing cards. Would you like to come?
   Chris: I'm flying to New York on Sunday.
   David: Are you seeing John there?
   Chris: Yes, we're meeting at the airport.

3. Philip: Mary and I are driving to Scotland next Wednesday.
   Mike: Are you staying in Edinburgh?
   Philip: No, we're visiting my mother in Aberdeen.

4. Paul: I'm starting a new job on Monday.
   Clive: Really? What is it?
   Paul: I'm selling cars. Do you need a new car?

gaps. Sometimes more than one answer is possible.
»

2 Remember to buy some stamps … you're in the post office.

1. I'm going to keep working until I finish this.
2. Remember to buy some stamps while/as soon as/when you're in the post office.
3. As soon as I speak to him on the phone tonight, I'll ask him.
4. We can go for a meal after we've seen the film.
5. I'll keep looking for it until I find it.
6. I'll wait for them until it gets dark, and then I'll leave.
7. Don't forget to lock the door when/before you go out.
8. As soon as I've found the information, I'll phone you.
9. We'll wait until it stops raining, and then we'll go out.
10. When/As soon as you see John, give him my regards.
11. Put in your application before the closing date arrives.
12. You shouldn't wait. You should reply as soon as you receive the invitation.
13. Book a table before/as soon as you go to the restaurant. It's often full.

Here are the completed dialogues using the Present Simple or will forms of the verbs provided:

A: Could you post this letter for me today, please?
B: Yes, I'll do it when I go to the shops.

1.
A: I might be late tonight.
B: OK. I'll wait until you arrive.

2.
A: I'm leaving next week.
B: I'll see you before you go, won't I?

3.
A: Have you decided what you're going to do at the weekend yet?
B: No, but I'll phone you as soon as I know what I'm going to do.

4.
A: Have you done that homework yet?
B: No, not yet. I'll do it when I have enough time.

5.
A: I don't want to go to that party tonight.
B: Well, I'm sure you'll enjoy it when you get there.

6.
A: Could you tell Tom to ring me, please?
B: Yes, I'll tell him when I see him tomorrow.

7. 
A: Mr Jackson isn't in at the moment.
B: I see. Well, I'll wait until he comes back.

8.
A: Have you booked a hotel in London yet?
B: No, but we'll book one before we go there.

9.
A: Don't forget to write to Peter.
B: OK. I'll do it as soon as I get home.

10.
A: Will you see Jack when you are in Madrid?
B: Yes, I hope so. I'll phone him when I arrive in Spain.

Complete the sentences using the Present Perfect or will forms of the verbs
in brackets ( ).
When you have written… (write) that letter, I'll poet… (post) it for you.
… (pay) the bill when I have borrowed some money from somebody.
2 When I've found a car that I want to buy, I … (ask) my bank to lend me the
money to buy it.
3
After the plane … (land), you may unfasten your safety belts.
4 When you …
… (check) all your answers, hand in your question paper.
5 I … (read) this book when I'm on holiday.
6 When I … (read) this magazine, I'll start work.

When you have written that letter, I'll post it for you.
1. I'll pay the bill when I have borrowed some money from somebody.
2. When I've found a car that I want to buy, I will ask my bank to lend me the money to buy it.
3. After the plane has landed, you may unfasten your safety belts.
4. When you have checked all your answers, hand in your question paper.
5. I will read this book when I'm on holiday.
6. When I have read this magazine, I'll start work.
7. You will feel better when you have had something to eat.
8. When you have finished your work, you can go home.
9. She will be pleased when she hears the news.
10. Let's go for a walk after we have had dinner.

Liz has come back to London from Holland. Her brother Tom has just met her at
Liverpool Street Station. In the sentences below, think about when the person
decides to do something. Put a tick if you think the phrase underlined is correct.
Otherwise write in the correct form of will or be going to.
Tom: Hi Liz. Do you want some tea or coffee after your journey?
Liz: Thanks. I'll ()… have a tea.
Tom: I'm going to (»)Il… carry your bag - you look tired. We'll (1)… go
to that cafe, over there. Here we are. So, welcome back to England. How was
Holland?
Liz: Well, it was great to have some time to think, and I've made some decisions. I'll
… talk to the boss tomorrow, and I'll (3)
… ask him if I can
move to another department.
Tom:
Good. I'm sure he'll (4)… give you what you want. How, would you like
something to eat?

The conversation between Tom and Liz includes both on-the-spot decisions and previously made decisions about future actions. These will affect whether "will" or "be going to" is the correct form to use. Here's the assessment based on the context:

Tom: Hi Liz. Do you want some tea or coffee after your journey?
Liz: Thanks. I'll have a tea. ✓ (On-the-spot decision)
Tom: I'm going to carry your bag - you look tired. ✓ (Looks like a decision made based on the situation)
We'll go to that cafe, over there. (This could go either way; it sounds spontaneous, so "will" might be fine, but if Tom decided before Liz arrived, then "we're going to" would be more appropriate.)
Here we are. So, welcome back to England. How was Holland?
Liz: Well, it was great to have some time to think, and I've made some decisions. I'll talk to the boss tomorrow, and I'll ask him if I can move to another department. ✓ (These are plans Liz made in the past, but using "will" is also correct since she is expressing her future intentions)
Tom: Good. I'm sure he'll give you what you want. ✓ (Tom is predicting the future based on his opinion)

Lastly for the food:
Tom: How would you like something to eat?
(The correct response depends on Liz's decision. If she decides at that moment she's hungry, "Yes, I'll have something to eat" is correct. If she had been planning to eat something after her journey already, "Yes, I'm going to have something to eat" would be correct.)

Use the words in brackets ( ) to write sentences using will and the Present Simple.
((To/help/us/when/he/come/home) Tom will help.us when he.comes home…
1
(I/buy/the tickets/before/l/go/to work) …
2
(As soon as/Henry/arrive/, we/have/something to eat) …
3 (The play/start/after/the music/stop)
4 (He/not/stop/until/he/finish/the job)
…
5 (When John/get/here, we/go/to the beach) …

1. I'll buy the tickets before I go to work.
2. As soon as Henry arrives, we'll have something to eat.
3. The play will start after the music stops.
4. He won't stop until he finishes the job.
5. When John gets here, we'll go to the beach.

Ann Jenkins is taking Tim to the airport on Monday morning.
On Monday evening, she is going to wash the car.

1. On Tuesday she is going to buy some stamps because in the evening she is going to write some letters.
2. She can't see anyone on Wednesday morning because she is taking the dog to the vet.
3. On Wednesday evening, she is going to tidy her flat.
4. On Thursday afternoon she is going to buy a new squash racket because she is playing squash with Mary on Friday morning.
5. On Friday afternoon she is going to do the shopping.
6. She is washing her hair on Saturday morning because she is meeting Tim at the airport at six o'clock.

London has had an underground train system since the nineteenth century. The London Underground started in 1863, when Victorian engineers and workers built the Metropolitan railway. This railway line went from Paddington Station to Farringdon Street Station, and steam engines pulled the coaches. Eleven more lines have opened since then. The world's first underground electric railway opened in 1890. This line went from the City of London to Stockwell in South London. The most modern line is the Jubilee line, which opened in 1979. Since the London Underground began, many other cities, such as New York and Moscow, have built their own systems.

Dennis: Have you read anything interesting lately?
Sarah: Yes, somebody lent me a novel last week and I really enjoyed it.

Dennis: What was it about?
Sarah: It was about a woman who goes to visit Australia. She likes to go everywhere alone. While she's traveling around on her own, something terrible happens to her. She loses everything - including her passport and all her money. She doesn't know anyone who can help her, and she's got nowhere to stay.

Dennis: What happens then?
Sarah: I'm not going to tell you anything else! You should read the book yourself.

Dennis: It sounds like a very depressing book! I'd prefer to read something funny.
Sarah: No, read it. It's great fun. And something wonderful happens at the end.
